request.question: Traction city is a prequel to a novella by Philip Reeve, what is the third book including the prequel?

title: Larklight
Here Lies Arthur
Mortal Engines Quartet
Starcross (novel)
A Web of Air
Traction City (novella)
Fever Crumb Series
Mortal Engines
Infernal Devices (Philip Reeve novel)
Mothstorm
sentences: Larklight, or the Revenge of the White Spiders!
or to Saturn's Rings and Back!
is a young adult novel by author Philip Reeve.
Illustrated by David Wyatt, it is the first book in the Larklight Trilogy.
Here Lies Arthur is a young-adult novel by Philip Reeve, published by Scholastic in 2007.

Set in fifth or sixth century Britain and the Anglo-Saxon invasion, it features a girl who participates in the deliberate construction of legendary King Arthur during the man's lifetime, orchestrated by a bard.
Reeve calls it a back-creation: not a genuine historical novel as it is not based on actual specific events; rather it is "back-created" from the legends, giving them a "realistic" origin.

The Mortal Engines Quartet is a tetralogy consisting of four novels written by the British author Philip Reeve: "Mortal Engines" (2001), "Predator's Gold" (2003), "Infernal Devices" (2005), and "A Darkling Plain" (2006).
It has also been referred to as the Predator Cities Quartet or the Hungry City Chronicles, although the author has objected to those names.
Starcross, or the Coming of the Moobs!
or Our Adventures in the Fourth Dimension!
is a young adult novel by Philip Reeve, released in October 2007.
Illustrated by David Wyatt, it is the second book in the "Larklight" trilogy, sequel to the 2006 novel "Larklight".
A Web of Air is a young adult post-apocalyptic science fiction novel by Philip Reeve.
The sequel to "Fever Crumb", it is the second book in the Mortal Engines Quartet prequel series.
It was published in April 2010.
Traction City is a novella by Philip Reeve and is a prequel to the Mortal Engines Quartet it was released as a flip book alongside Chris Priestlys' teachers tales of terror for World Book Day.
The Fever Crumb Series is the title of a series of novels written by British author, Philip Reeve, and is the prequel series to his critically acclaimed "Mortal Engines Quartet".
As of 2015 the series consists of three books: "Fever Crumb" (2009), "A Web of Air" (2010), and "Scrivener's Moon" (2011).
The books follow a young woman known as Fever Crumb, and her adventures set around the time of the creation of the first traction cities that become so prevalent in the Mortal Engines series.
Reeve stated in 2011 that he had always envisaged a quartet for the series, assuring readers of a fourth installment.
Mortal Engines is the first of four novels in Philip Reeve's quartet of the same name.
The book focuses on a futuristic, steampunk version of London, now a giant machine striving to survive on a world running out of resources.
The book has won a Nestlé Smarties Book Prize and was shortlisted for the 2002 Whitbread Award.
Infernal Devices is the third of four novels in Philip Reeve's children's series, the "Mortal Engines Quartet".
Mothstorm, or the Horror from Beyond Uranus Georgium Sidus!
or a Tale of Two Shapers is a young adult novel by Philip Reeve, released in October 2008.
Illustrated by David Wyatt, it is the third book in the Larklight Trilogy, sequel to the 2007 novel "Starcross".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

hop1_docs: Traction City (novella) | Traction City is a novella by Philip Reeve and is a prequel to the Mortal Engines Quartet it was released as a flip book alongside Chris Priestlys' teachers tales of terror for World Book Day.

Fever Crumb Series | The Fever Crumb Series is the title of a series of novels written by British author, Philip Reeve, and is the prequel series to his critically acclaimed "Mortal Engines Quartet".  As of 2015 the series consists of three books: "Fever Crumb" (2009), "A Web of Air" (2010), and "Scrivener's Moon" (2011).  The books follow a young woman known as Fever Crumb, and her adventures set around the time of the creation of the first traction cities that become so prevalent in the Mortal Engines series.  Reeve stated in 2011 that he had always envisaged a quartet for the series, assuring readers of a fourth installment.

A Web of Air | A Web of Air is a young adult post-apocalyptic science fiction novel by Philip Reeve.  The sequel to "Fever Crumb", it is the second book in the Mortal Engines Quartet prequel series.  It was published in April 2010.

Fever Crumb | Fever Crumb is a young adult post-apocalyptic science fiction novel by Philip Reeve, published in 2009.  Sequels called "A Web of Air" (2010) and "Scrivener's Moon" (2011) follow.  The books of the Fever Crumb Series are prequels to the Mortal Engines Quartet series of novels by the same author.

Hard Choices (graphic novella) | Hard Choices 'What happened on Algol?'  is a 32-page graphic novella prequel to "" written by Dan Abnett, released as part of the Special Edition DVD on 29 November 2010.

Alice series | The "Alice" series is a young adult book series written by Phyllis Reynolds Naylor.  There are three prequels to this series.  The first one, "Starting with Alice", describes Alice in third grade.  "Alice in Blunderland" is Alice in fourth grade.  The final prequel, "Lovingly Alice", follows Alice through the troubles of fifth grade.  The first book (after the prequels) is "The Agony of Alice".

Once Upon a Time in the North | Once Upon a Time in the North, a fantasy novella by Philip Pullman (first published on 3 April 2008 in the United Kingdom) functions as a prequel to Pullman's "His Dark Materials" trilogy.  The premise of the story involves the meeting of Iorek Byrnison and Lee Scoresby:

- From passage [1]: "Traction City is a novella" and "is a prequel to the Mortal Engines Quartet."
- From passages [2] and [4]: the Fever Crumb Series is a "prequel series to ... 'Mortal Engines Quartet'" and "consists of three books: 'Fever Crumb' (2009), 'A Web of Air' (2010), and 'Scrivener's Moon' (2011)."
- From passage [3]: "'A Web of Air' ... is the second book" (published April 2010).
- Missing / ambiguous: it is not stated whether "Traction City" is counted as part of the Fever Crumb Series or the same prequel sequence—bridge entity: Traction City (its inclusion/chronology relative to the Fever Crumb books is unknown).
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1. "Traction City (novella)" page — check lede/infobox and Publication/Overview for series membership or chronology.
  2. "Fever Crumb Series" page (or "Fever Crumb" novel page) — inspect lede/infobox and "Books"/"Series" section to confirm ordered list and that "Scrivener's Moon" is the third.
  3. "Scrivener's Moon" page — lede/infobox to confirm publication year and position in series.
  4. "Mortal Engines" (or "Mortal Engines Quartet") page — "Prequels" or "Related works" section to list all prequel novellas/short works and their order.

The Complete Crumb Comics | The Complete Crumb Comics is an award-winning series of collections from Fantagraphics Books which was intended to reproduce the entire body of American cartoonist and comic book artist/writer Robert Crumb's comics work in chronological order, beginning with his fanzine work from as early as 1958.

A Web of Air | A Web of Air is a young adult post-apocalyptic science fiction novel by Philip Reeve.  The sequel to "Fever Crumb", it is the second book in the Mortal Engines Quartet prequel series.  It was published in April 2010.

Santa Maria Novella | Santa Maria Novella is a church in Florence, Italy, situated just across from the main railway station named after it.  Chronologically, it is the first great basilica in Florence, and is the city's principal Dominican church.

The Werewolf of Fever Swamp | The Werewolf of Fever Swamp is the fourteenth book in "Goosebumps", the series of children's horror fiction novellas created and authored by R. L. Stine.  The story follows Grady Tucker, who moves into a new house next to the Fever Swamp with his family.  After a swamp deer is murdered, his father believes Grady's dog is responsible, but Grady is convinced a werewolf is the culprit.  One reviewer felt the book built up suspense by hiding the identity of the werewolf until the end.
response.summary_2: Traction City is a novella by Philip Reeve and is a prequel to the Mortal Engines Quartet. Fever Crumb Series comprises three prequel books — Fever Crumb, A Web of Air, and Scrivener's Moon — with Scrivener's Moon (2011) being the third.
response.answer:
- If Traction City is NOT counted as part of the Fever Crumb sequence: the third book is Scrivener's Moon (2011).
- If Traction City IS counted as an included prequel (placed before the three Fever Crumb novels): the third work would be A Web of Air (2010).
